During this period, Columbia scored a Top 40 hit with the [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") single "[Another Brick in the Wall](/wiki/Another_Brick_in_the_Wall "Another Brick in the Wall")", and its parent album *[The Wall](/wiki/The_Wall "The Wall")* would spend four months at No. 1 on the *Billboard* LP chart in early 1980, but few in the industry knew that Dick Asher was in fact using the single as a covert experiment to test the extent of the pernicious influence of The Network – by *not* paying them to promote the new Pink Floyd single.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The results were immediate and troubling – not one of the major radio stations in Los Angeles would program the record, despite the fact that the group was in town, performing the first seven concerts on their elaborate [The Wall Tour](/wiki/The_Wall_Tour "The Wall Tour") at the [Los Angeles Memorial Sports Arena](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Sports_Arena "Los Angeles Memorial Sports Arena") to rave reviews and sold-out crowds. Asher was already worried about the growing power of The Network, and the fact it operated entirely outside the control of the label, but he was profoundly dismayed to realize that "The Network" was in effect a huge [extortion](/wiki/Extortion "Extortion") racket, and that the operation could well be linked to organized crime – a concern vehemently dismissed by Yetnikoff, who resolutely defended the "indies" and declared them to be "mensches". But Dick Asher now knew that The Network's real power lay in their ability to *prevent* records from being picked up by radio, and as an experienced media lawyer and a loyal CBS employee, he was also acutely aware that this could become a new [payola](/wiki/Payola "Payola") scandal which had the potential to engulf the entire CBS corporation, and that the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) could even revoke CBS' all-important broadcast licenses if the corporation was found to be involved in any illegality.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDannen19914–27-73)

### The 1980s and sale to Sony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: The 1980s and sale to Sony")]

The structure of US Columbia remained the same until 1980, when it spun off the classical/Broadway unit, Columbia Masterworks Records, into a separate imprint, CBS Masterworks Records.

In 1988, the CBS Records Group, including the Columbia Records unit, was acquired by [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), which re-christened the parent division [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment") in 1991. As Sony only had a temporary license on the CBS Records name, it then acquired the rights to the Columbia trademarks ([Columbia Graphophone](/wiki/Columbia_Graphophone "Columbia Graphophone")) outside the U.S., Canada, Spain (trademark owned by [BMG](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group "Bertelsmann Music Group")) and Japan (Nippon Columbia) from [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI"), a firm which generally had not used them since the early 1970s. The CBS Records label was officially renamed Columbia Records on January 1, 1991, worldwide except Spain (where Sony got the rights in 2004 by forming a joint venture with BMG[[74]](#cite_note-74)) and Japan.[[75]](#cite_note-75) CBS Masterworks Records was renamed [Sony Classical Records](/wiki/Sony_Classical_Records "Sony Classical Records"). In December 2006, [CBS Corporation](/wiki/CBS_Corporation "CBS Corporation") revived the [CBS Records](/wiki/CBS_Records_(2006) "CBS Records (2006)") name for a new minor label closely linked with its television properties (coincidentally, the new CBS Records is currently distributed by another Sony Music division, [RED Distribution](/wiki/RED_Distribution "RED Distribution")).

### The 1990s–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: The 1990s–present")]

American [talent manager](/wiki/Talent_manager "Talent manager") Will Botwin was recruited in 1996 to serve as a senior vice president. In 1998, he was appointed executive vice president/general manager and worked closely with many of the label's established artists, and newer performers such as [Microdisney](/wiki/Microdisney "Microdisney") and [Five For Fighting](/wiki/Five_For_Fighting "Five For Fighting").[[76]](#cite_note-:0-76) In 2002, Botwin was promoted to president of the Columbia Records Group;[[77]](#cite_note-77) he was appointed chairman in 2005.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In 2006, he left Columbia to head [Red Light Management](/wiki/Red_Light_Management "Red Light Management") and its associated [ATO Records](/wiki/ATO_Records "ATO Records").[[76]](#cite_note-:0-76)

Columbia Records remains a premier subsidiary label of [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment"). In 2009, during the re-consolidation of Sony Music, Columbia was partnered with its [Epic Records](/wiki/Epic_Records "Epic Records") sister to form the [Columbia/Epic Label Group](/wiki/Columbia/Epic_Label_Group "Columbia/Epic Label Group")[[79]](#cite_note-79) under which it operated as an imprint. In July 2011, as part of further corporate restructuring, Epic was split from the Columbia/Epic Group as Epic took in multiple artists from Jive Records.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

As of December 2025, Columbia Records has more than hundred artists, ranging various genres. Some of the artists includes, [Addison Rae](/wiki/Addison_Rae "Addison Rae"), [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele"), [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel"), [Hozier](/wiki/Hozier "Hozier") and many more.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

As of January 2, 2018, [Ron Perry](/wiki/Ron_Perry_(music) "Ron Perry (music)") has been named as the chairman and CEO of Columbia Records.[[82]](#cite_note-New_York_Business_Journal-82) In January 2023, Jennifer Mallory was appointed as president.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

## Logos and branding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Logos and branding")]

The acquisition of rights to the Columbia trademarks by EMI (including the "Magic Notes" logo) presented the company with a dilemma regarding which logo to use. For much of the 1990s, Columbia released its albums with the "walking eye" logo absent, just the "COLUMBIA" word mark in the [Bodoni](/wiki/Bodoni "Bodoni") Classic Bold typeface.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Columbia experimented with bringing back the "Notes and Mic" logo but without the CBS mark on the microphone. That logo is currently used in the "Columbia Jazz" series of jazz releases and reissues.[[85]](#cite_note-85) A modified "Magic Notes" logo is found on the logo for [Sony Classical](/wiki/Sony_Classical "Sony Classical"). In mid to late 1999, it was eventually decided that the "Walking Eye" (previously the CBS Records logo outside North America) would be Columbia's logo, with the retained Columbia word mark design, throughout the world except in Japan where [Nippon Columbia](/wiki/Nippon_Columbia "Nippon Columbia") has the rights to the Columbia trademark to this day and continues to use the "Magic Notes" logo. In Japan, CBS/Sony Records was renamed Sony Records in 1991 and stopped using the "Walking Eye" logo in 1998.

## Affiliated labels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Affiliated labels")]

### Columbia Label Group (UK)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Columbia Label Group (UK)")]

In January 2006, Sony BMG UK split operations into RCA Label Group, mainly dealing with pop and R&B, and Columbia Label Group, mainly dealing with rock, dance and alternative music. Domestic artists include [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris"), [George Ezra](/wiki/George_Ezra "George Ezra"), [Central Cee](/wiki/Central_Cee "Central Cee") and [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams"). Subsidiary labels include Ministry of Sound Records and Room Two Recordings.

### American Recording Company (ARC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: American Recording Company (ARC)")]

In August 1978, [Maurice White](/wiki/Maurice_White "Maurice White"), founder and leader of the band [Earth, Wind & Fire](/wiki/Earth,_Wind_%26_Fire "Earth, Wind & Fire"), re-launched the [American Recording Company](/wiki/American_Record_Corporation "American Record Corporation") (ARC). Among its artists were R&B and pop singer [Deniece Williams](/wiki/Deniece_Williams "Deniece Williams"), jazz-fusion group [Weather Report](/wiki/Weather_Report "Weather Report"), and R&B trio [the Emotions](/wiki/The_Emotions "The Emotions").[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) Since the 1940s, Columbia has also re-issued thousands of 1930s records issued on ARC labels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Aware Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Aware Records")]

In 1997, Columbia made an affiliation with the label [Aware Records](/wiki/Aware_Records "Aware Records") to distribute Aware's artists' music. In 2002, Columbia and Aware accepted the option to continue this relationship.

### Columbia Nashville

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Columbia Nashville")]

In 2007, Columbia formed Columbia Nashville, which is part of [Sony Music Nashville](/wiki/Sony_Music_Nashville "Sony Music Nashville"). This gave Columbia Nashville complete autonomy and managerial separation from Columbia in New York City. Columbia had given its [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") department semi-autonomy for many years and through the 1950s, had a 20,000 series catalog for country music singles while the rest of Columbia's output of singles had a 30,000, then 40,000 series catalog number.

In 2009, Columbia Nashville became part of [Sony Music Nashville](/wiki/Sony_Music_Nashville "Sony Music Nashville") under the [Sony Group Corporation](/wiki/Sony_Group_Corporation "Sony Group Corporation") umbrella through [Sony Music Group](/wiki/Sony_Music_Group "Sony Music Group").

## Recording studios

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Recording studios")]

Columbia Records operated [recording studios](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio"), the most notable of which were in New York City, Nashville, Hollywood and San Francisco. Columbia's first recording studio was established in 1913, after the company moved into the [Woolworth Building](/wiki/Woolworth_Building "Woolworth Building") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), the tallest building in the world at the time.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In 1917, Columbia used this studio to make one of the earliest jazz records, by the [Original Dixieland Jass Band](/wiki/Original_Dixieland_Jass_Band "Original Dixieland Jass Band").[[91]](#cite_note-Cogan-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### 7th Avenue, New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 7th Avenue, New York")]

In 1939, Columbia established **Studio A** at 799 Seventh Avenue in New York City.[[22]](#cite_note-StoryColumbia-22) This studio – well-known for its use by [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") in the 1960s – was purchased in 1967 by Jack Arnold and [Phil Ramone](/wiki/Phil_Ramone "Phil Ramone")'s [A & R Recording](/wiki/A_%26_R_Recording "A & R Recording"), and became Studios A-1 and A-2 for A & R. The building was demolished in 1983 to make way for the [Equitable Tower](/wiki/Equitable_Tower "Equitable Tower").

### 52nd Street, New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 52nd Street, New York")]

Main article: [CBS Studio Building](/wiki/CBS_Studio_Building "CBS Studio Building")

**Studio B** and **Studio E** were located in the [CBS Studio Building](/wiki/CBS_Studio_Building "CBS Studio Building") at 49 East 52nd Street in New York City – on the second and fifth floor, respectively.[[93]](#cite_note-SIMONS-93)

### 30th Street, New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 30th Street, New York")]

Main article: [CBS 30th Street Studio](/wiki/CBS_30th_Street_Studio "CBS 30th Street Studio")

In 1948, Columbia built additional new studios at 207 East 30th Street in Manhattan's Murray Hill district, naming them **Studio C** and **Studio D**. This complex, nicknamed "The Church" due to it having been built within an 1875 building that was originally constructed as a Christian church, was considered by some in the music industry to be the best-sounding room of its time, and many consider it to have been the greatest recording studio in history.[[93]](#cite_note-SIMONS-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) CBS never took up the option to buy the building outright, giving up its lease and closing the studio in 1981. In spite of the building's inherent heritage status and its cultural significance, it was sold to developers in 1985, demolished, and replaced by a high-rise apartment complex.

### Columbia Square, Hollywood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Columbia Square, Hollywood")]

See also: [CBS Columbia Square](/wiki/CBS_Columbia_Square "CBS Columbia Square")

In 1961, Columbia Records renovated and repurposed CBS Radio Studio A at the company's Columbia Square complex at 6121 Sunset Boulevard in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), California. Columbia utilized the studio for recording and mastering services until its closure in 1972.

### Columbia Recording Studios, Chicago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Columbia Recording Studios, Chicago")]

Columbia maintained one of its principal recording studios in Chicago (c.1939-c.1974). While under the ownership of CBS Inc., the label’s facilities were always housed alongside the network’s radio and television stations in Chicago (WBBM-AM/FM/TV). Before 1956, the studios operated out of the Wrigley Building; after that year, they moved to 630 North McClurg Court. The site was also commonly known as “CBS Studios, Chicago.”[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Columbia Studios, Nashville

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Columbia Studios, Nashville")]

See also: [Bradley Studios](/wiki/Bradley_Studios "Bradley Studios")

In 1962, Columbia Records purchased the [Bradley Studios](/wiki/Bradley_Studios "Bradley Studios") in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") (also known as the Quonset Hut), the first recording studio in what would become Nashville's [Music Row](/wiki/Music_Row "Music Row") district when first established by [Harold](/wiki/Harold_Bradley_(guitarist) "Harold Bradley (guitarist)") and [Owen Bradley](/wiki/Owen_Bradley "Owen Bradley") in 1954. Renamed **Columbia Studios**, the company replaced Bradley Studio A with a building containing a newer, larger studio A, mixing and mastering studios, and administrative offices. Columbia operated these studios from 1962 through 1982, when they were converted into office space.[[96]](#cite_note-Skates-96) Philanthropist Mike Curb bought the structure in 2006 and restored it; it is now a recording classroom for [Belmont University](/wiki/Belmont_University "Belmont University")'s Mike Curb College of Entertainment & Music Business.[[96]](#cite_note-Skates-96)

### Liederkranz Hall Studio, New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Columbia_Records&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Liederkranz Hall Studio, New York")]

Columbia also recorded in the highly respected Liederkranz Hall, at 111 East 58th Street between Park and Lexington Avenues, in New York City, it was built by and formerly belonged to a German cultural and musical society, [The Liederkranz Society](/wiki/Liederkranz_of_the_City_of_New_York "Liederkranz of the City of New York"), and used as a recording studio ([Victor](/wiki/Victor_Talking_Machine_Company "Victor Talking Machine Company") also recorded in Liederkranz Hall in the late 1920s).[[93]](#cite_note-SIMONS-93)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) The producer Morty Palitz had been instrumental in convincing Columbia Records to begin to use the Liederkranz Hall studio for recording music, additionally convincing the conductor [Andre Kostelanetz](/wiki/Andre_Kostelanetz "Andre Kostelanetz") to make some of the first recordings in Liederkranz Hall which until then had only been used for [CBS Symphony](/wiki/Columbia_Symphony_Orchestra "Columbia Symphony Orchestra") radio shows.[[100]](#cite_note-100) In 1949, the large Liederkranz Hall space was physically rearranged to create four television studios.[[93]](#cite_note-SIMONS-93)[[101]](#cite_note-KAHN2001-101)
